I. A public place.
Scene II. A Street.
Scene III. Room in Capulet’s House.
Scene IV. A Street.
Scene V. A Hall in Capulet’s House.

ACT II
CHORUS.
Scene I. An open place adjoining Capulet’s Garden.
Scene II. Capulet’s Garden.
Scene III. Friar Lawrence’s Cell.
Scene IV. A Street.
Scene V. Capulet’s Garden.
Scene VI. Friar Lawrence’s Cell.

ACT III
Scene I. A public Place.
Scene II. A Room in Capulet’s House.
Scene III. Friar Lawrence’s cell.
Scene IV. A Room in Capulet’s House.
Scene V. An open Gallery to Juliet’s Chamber, overlooking the Garden.

ACT IV
Scene I. Friar Lawrence’s Cell.
Scene II. Hall in Capulet’s House.
Scene III. Juliet’s Chamber.
Scene IV. Hall in Capulet’s House.
Scene V. Juliet’s Chamber; Juliet on the bed.

ACT V
Scene I. Mantua. A Street.
Scene II. Friar Lawrence’s Cell.
Scene III. A churchyard; in it a Monument belonging to the Capulets.




 Dramatis Personæ

ESCALUS, Prince of Verona.
MERCUTIO, kinsman to the Prince, and friend to Romeo.
PARIS, a young Nobleman, kinsman to the Prince.
Page to Paris.

MONTAGUE, head of a Veronese family at feud with the Capulets.
LADY MONTAGUE, wife to Montague.
ROMEO, son to Montague.
BENVOLIO, nephew to Montague, and friend to Romeo.
ABRAM, servant to Montague.
BALTHASAR, servant to Romeo.

CAPULET, head of a Veronese family at feud with the Montagues.
LADY CAPULET, wife to Capulet.
JULIET, daughter to Capulet.
TYBALT, nephew to Lady Capulet.
CAPULET’S COUSIN, an old man.
NURSE to Juliet.
PETER, servant to Juliet’s Nurse.
SAMPSON, servant to Capulet.
GREGORY, servant to Capulet.
Servants.

FRIAR LAWRENCE, a Franciscan.
FRIAR JOHN, of the same Order.
An Apothecary.
CHORUS.
Three Musicians.
An Officer.
Citizens of Verona; several Men and Women, relations to both houses;
Maskers, Guards, Watchmen and Attendants.

SCENE. During the greater part of the Play in Verona; once, in the
Fifth Act, at Mantua.




THE PROLOGUE


 Enter Chorus.

CHORUS.
Two households, both alike in dignity,
In fair Verona, where we lay our scene,
From ancient grudge break to new mutiny,
Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean.
From forth the fatal loins of these two foes
A pair of star-cross’d lovers take their life;
Whose misadventur’d piteous overthrows
Doth with their death bury their parents’ strife.
The fearful passage of their death-mark’d love,
And the continuance of their parents’ rage,
Which, but their children’s end, nought could remove,
Is now the two hours’ traffic of our stage;
The which, if you with patient ears attend,
What here shall miss, our toil shall strive to mend.

 [_Exit._]




ACT I

SCENE I. A public place.


 Enter Sampson and Gregory armed with swords and bucklers.

SAMPSON.
Gregory, on my word, we’ll not carry coals.

GREGORY.
No, for then we should be colliers.

SAMPSON.
I mean, if we be in choler, we’ll draw.

GREGORY.
Ay, while you live, draw your neck out o’ the collar.

SAMPSON.
I strike quickly, being moved.

GREGORY.
But thou art not quickly moved to strike.

SAMPSON.
A dog of the house of Montague moves me.

GREGORY.
To move is to stir; and to be valiant is to stand: therefore, if thou
art moved, thou runn’st away.

SAMPSON.
A dog of that house shall move me to stand.
I will take the wall of any man or maid of Montague’s.

GREGORY.
That shows thee a weak slave, for the weakest goes to the wall.

SAMPSON.
True, and therefore women, being the weaker vessels, are ever thrust to
the wall: therefore I will push Montague’s men from the wall, and
thrust his maids to the wall.

GREGORY.
The quarrel is between our masters and us their men.

SAMPSON.
’Tis all one, I will show myself a tyrant: when I have fought with the
men I will be civil with the maids, I will cut off their heads.

GREGORY.
The heads of the maids?

SAMPSON.
Ay, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads; take it in what sense
thou wilt.

GREGORY.
They must take it in sense that feel it.

SAMPSON.
Me they shall feel while I am able to stand: and ’tis known I am a
pretty piece of flesh.

GREGORY.
’Tis well thou art not fish; if thou hadst, thou hadst been poor John.
Draw thy tool; here comes of the house of Montagues.

 Enter Abram and Balthasar.

SAMPSON.
My naked weapon is out: quarrel, I will back thee.

GREGORY.
How? Turn thy back and run?

SAMPSON.
Fear me not.

GREGORY.
No, marry; I fear thee!

SAMPSON.
Let us take the law of our sides; let them begin.

GREGORY.
I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it as they list.

SAMPSON.
Nay, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at them, which is disgrace to
them if they bear it.

ABRAM.
Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

SAMPSON.
I do bite my thumb, sir.

ABRAM.
Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

SAMPSON.
Is the law of our side if I say ay?

GREGORY.
No.

SAMPSON.
No sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir; but I bite my thumb, sir.

GREGORY.
Do you quarrel, sir?

ABRAM.
Quarrel, sir? No, sir.

SAMPSON.
But if you do, sir, I am for you. I serve as good a man as you.

ABRAM.
No better.

SAMPSON.
Well, sir.

 Enter Benvolio.

GREGORY.
Say better; here comes one of my master’s kinsmen.

SAMPSON.
Yes, better, sir.

ABRAM.
You lie.

SAMPSON.
Draw, if you be men. Gregory, remember thy washing blow.

 [_They fight._]

BENVOLIO.
Part, fools! put up your swords, you know not what you do.

 [_Beats down their swords._]

 Enter Tybalt.

TYBALT.
What, art thou drawn among these heartless hinds?
Turn thee Benvolio, look upon thy death.

BENVOLIO.
I do but keep the peace, put up thy sword,
Or manage it to part these men with me.

TYBALT.
What, drawn, and talk of peace? I hate the word
As I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee:
Have at thee, coward.

 [_They fight._]

 Enter three or four Citizens with clubs.

FIRST CITIZEN.
Clubs, bills and partisans! Strike! Beat them down!
Down with the Capulets! Down with the Montagues!

 Enter Capulet in his gown, and Lady Capulet.

CAPULET.
What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!

LADY CAPULET.
A crutch, a crutch! Why call you for a sword?

CAPULET.
My sword, I say! Old Montague is come,
And flourishes his blade in spite of me.

 Enter Montague and his Lady Montague.

MONTAGUE.
Thou villain Capulet! Hold me not, let me go.

LADY MONTAGUE.
Thou shalt not stir one foot to seek a foe.

 Enter Prince Escalus, with Attendants.

PRINCE.
Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace,
Profaners of this neighbour-stained steel,—
Will they not hear? What, ho! You men, you beasts,
That quench the fire of your pernicious rage
With purple fountains issuing from your veins,
On pain of torture, from those bloody hands
Throw your mistemper’d weapons to the ground
And hear the sentence of your moved prince.
Three civil brawls, bred of an airy word,
By thee, old Capulet, and Montague,
Have thrice disturb’d the quiet of our streets,
And made Verona’s ancient citizens
Cast by their grave beseeming ornaments,
To wield old partisans, in hands as old,
Canker’d with peace, to part your canker’d hate.
If ever you disturb our streets again,
Your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace.
For this time all the rest depart away:
You, Capulet, shall go along with me,
And Montague, come you this afternoon,
To know our farther pleasure in this case,
To old Free-town, our common judgement-place.
Once more, on pain of death, all men depart.

 [_Exeunt Prince and Attendants; Capulet, Lady Capulet, Tybalt,
 Citizens and Servants._]

MONTAGUE.
Who set this ancient quarrel new abroach?
Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?

BENVOLIO.
Here were the servants of your adversary
And yours, close fighting ere I did approach.
I drew to part them, in the instant came
The fiery Tybalt, with his sword prepar’d,
Which, as he breath’d defiance to my ears,
He swung about his head, and cut the winds,
Who nothing hurt withal, hiss’d him in scorn.
While we were interchanging thrusts and blows
Came more and more, and fought on part and part,
Till the Prince came, who parted either part.

LADY MONTAGUE.
O where is Romeo, saw you him today?
Right glad I am he was not at this fray.

BENVOLIO.
Madam, an hour before the worshipp’d sun
Peer’d forth the golden window of the east,
A troubled mind drave me to walk abroad,
Where underneath the grove of sycamore
That westward rooteth from this city side,
So early walking did I see your son.
Towards him I made, but he was ware of me,
And stole into the covert of the wood.
I, measuring his affections by my own,
Which then most sought where most might not be found,
Being one too many by my weary self,
Pursu’d my humour, not pursuing his,
And gladly shunn’d who gladly fled from me.

MONTAGUE.
Many a morning hath he there been seen,
With tears augmenting the fresh morning’s dew,
Adding to clouds more clouds with his deep sighs;
But all so soon as the all-cheering sun
Should in the farthest east begin to draw
The shady curtains from Aurora’s bed,
Away from light steals home my heavy son,
And private in his chamber pens himself,
Shuts up his windows, locks fair daylight out
And makes himself an artificial night.
Black and portentous must this humour prove,
Unless good counsel may the cause remove.

BENVOLIO.
My noble uncle, do you know the cause?

MONTAGUE.
I neither know it nor can learn of him.

BENVOLIO.
Have you importun’d him by any means?

MONTAGUE.
Both by myself and many other friends;
But he, his own affections’ counsellor,
Is to himself—I will not say how true—
But to himself so secret and so close,
So far from sounding and discovery,
As is the bud bit with an envious worm
Ere he can spread his sweet leaves to the air,
Or dedicate his beauty to the sun.
Could we but learn from whence his sorrows grow,
We would as willingly give cure as know.

 Enter Romeo.

BENVOLIO.
See, where he comes. So please you step aside;
I’ll know his grievance or be much denied.

MONTAGUE.
I would thou wert so happy by thy stay
To hear true shrift. Come, madam, let’s away,

 [_Exeunt Montague and Lady Montague._]

BENVOLIO.
Good morrow, cousin.

ROMEO.
Is the day so young?

BENVOLIO.
But new struck nine.

ROMEO.
Ay me, sad hours seem long.
Was that my father that went hence so fast?

BENVOLIO.
It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo’s hours?

ROMEO.
Not having that which, having, makes them short.

BENVOLIO.
In love?

ROMEO.
Out.

BENVOLIO.
Of love?

ROMEO.
Out of her favour where I am in love.

BENVOLIO.
Alas that love so gentle in his view,
Should be so tyrannous and rough in proof.

ROMEO.
Alas that love, whose view is muffled still,
Should, without eyes, see pathways to his will!
Where shall we dine? O me! What fray was here?
Yet tell me not, for I have heard it all.
Here’s much to do with hate, but more with love:
Why, then, O brawling love! O loving hate!
O anything, of nothing first create!
O heavy lightness! serious vanity!
Misshapen chaos of well-seeming forms!
Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire, sick health!
Still-waking sleep, that is not what it is!
This love feel I, that feel no love in this.
Dost thou not laugh?

BENVOLIO.
No coz, I rather weep.

ROMEO.
Good heart, at what?

BENVOLIO.
At thy good heart’s oppression.

ROMEO.
Why such is love’s transgression.
Griefs of mine own lie heavy in my breast,
Which thou wilt propagate to have it prest
With more of thine. This love that thou hast shown
Doth add more grief to too much of mine own.
Love is a smoke made with the fume of sighs;
Being purg’d, a fire sparkling in lovers’ eyes;
Being vex’d, a sea nourish’d with lovers’ tears:
What is it else? A madness most discreet,
A choking gall, and a preserving sweet.
Farewell, my coz.

 [_Going._]

BENVOLIO.
Soft! I will go

pt-br.title: Romeu e Julieta
pt-br.synopsis: Em Verona, o amor floresce com a urgência fatal de um verão escaldante. Romeu e Julieta, jovens amantes das famílias rivais Montéquio e Capuleto, encontram no olhar um destino selado. Mas paixão não anula ódio. Entre banquetes estrondosos, confrontos sangrentos e o peso ancestral da rivalidade familiar, esse amor jovem é forçado a desafiar os deuses. Uma tragédia inevitável se desenrola: cartas perdidas, mal-entendidos fatais e um beijo que promete eternidade culminam em uma cena de desespero onde dois corações decidem morrer para viver juntos. Romeu e Julieta: o romance épico onde a paixão é mais perigosa arma contra a fúria do destino.

**LOCAL.** Durante a maior parte da peça em Verona; uma vez, no Quinto Ato, em Mântua.

***

# O PRÓLOGO

*Entra o CÓRUS.*

**CÓRUS.**
Duas casas, ambas de igual dignidade,
Em bela Verona, onde cenamos esta cena,
De antiga inimizade quebra para nova mutinidade,
Onde sangue civil torna as mãos civis impuras.
Dos fatais lombos destes dois inimigos,
Um par de amantes cruzados tira a vida;
Cujos tristes e desaventurados derrubamentos
Enterram com sua morte a discórdia dos pais.

A temível passagem de seu amor marcado pelo destino,
E a permanência da fúria de seus genitores,
Que, senão com o fim de seus filhos, nada poderia remover,
É agora o trânsito em nossas duas horas no palco;
O qual, se ouvirdes com pacientes ouvidos,
O que aqui faltar, nosso trabalho tentará consertar.

[*Sai.*]

# ATO I

**CENA I.** Um lugar público.

*Entram Sampson e Gregory armados com espadas e escudo.*

**SAMPSON.**
Gregory, por minha palavra, não carregaremos carvão.

**GREGORY.**
Não, pois assim seremos carpinteiros (ou *carbonários*).

**SAMPSON.**
Quero dizer, se estivermos em cólera, desembainharemos.

**GREGORY.**
Ah, enquanto viveres, tira o pescoço do colarinho!

**SAMPSON.**
Acerto depressa, sendo movido.

**GREGORY.**
Mas tu não te moves depressa para golpear.

**SAMPSON.**
Um cão da casa de Montecchio me move.

**GREGORY.**
Mover-se é agitar; e ser valente é ficar: portanto, se és movido, corres embora.

**SAMPSON.**
Um cão daquela casa me moverá a permanecer.
Vou tomar o muro de qualquer homem ou moça de Montecchio.

**GREGORY.**
Isso mostra um servo fraco, pois o mais fraco vai para o muro.

**SAMPSON.**

**GREGORY.**

**SAMPSON.**

**GREGORY.**

**SAMPSON.**
Sim, as cabeças das moças, ou suas virgindades; pega como quiseres.

**GREGORY.**
É preciso que peguem no sentido que sentirem.

**SAMPSON.**

**GREGORY.**

*Entram Abram e Balthasar.*

pt-pt.title: Romeu e Julieta
pt-pt.synopsis: Numa Verona fervilhante de orgulho familiar, o amor floresce num embate trágico. Romeu, jovem apaixonado e impulsivo, encontra em Julieta o fulgor que ilumina a sua existência. Contudo, os ódios ancestrais entre as Casas Montéquio e Capuleto ameaçam consumir este romance puro. Quando segredos e mal-entendidos se tecem numa tapeçaria de destino cruel, os dois amantes lutam contra um mundo implacável, cujas paixões incandescentes pavimentam o caminho para uma consumação sublime... e fatal.

Enterram com a morte a discórdia dos seus pais.
A temida passagem do amor marcado pelo destino,
E a persistência da fúria dos seus progenitores,
Que, senão com o fim de os filhos, nada poderia remover,
É agora o trânsito das nossas duas horas no palco;
O qual, se ouvirdes com pacientes ouvidos,
Tentar-se-á consertar o que aqui falhar.

[_Sai._]

***

**ATO I**

**CENA I.** Um lugar público.

Entram Sampson e Gregory armados com espadas e escudos.

**SAMPSON.**
Gregory, por minha palavra, não carregaremos carvão.

**GREGORY.**
Não, pois assim seríamos lenhadores.

**SAMPSON.**
Quero dizer, se estivermos em cólera, desembainharemos.

**GREGORY.**
Sim, enquanto vives, tira o pescoço do colarinho (*do aperto*).

**SAMPSON.**
Ataquei depressa, sendo movido.

**GREGORY.**
Mas tu não te moves depressa para atacar.

**SAMPSON.**
Um cão da casa de Montecchio me move.

**GREGORY.**
Mover-se é agitar; e ser valente é ficar firme: portanto, se te moves, fuges.

**SAMPSON.**
Um cão dessa casa moverá para que eu fique firme.
Vou tomar o muro de qualquer homem ou moça dos Montcchio.

**GREGORY.**
Isso mostra-te um servo fraco, pois o mais fraco vai para o muro.

**SAMPSON.**
Verdade, e por isso as mulheres, sendo os vasos mais frágeis, são sempre empurradas para
o muro: portanto eu empurrei os homens de Montecchio para o muro, e
empurro suas moças para o muro.

**GREGORY.**
A briga é entre os nossos mestres e nós, seus homens.

**SAMPSON.**
É tudo um só, mostrarei ser um tirano: quando tiver lutado com
os homens serei civilizado com as donzelas, cortarei-lhes a cabeça.

**GREGORY.**
As cabeças das moças?

**SAMPSON.**
Sim, as cabeças das moças, ou suas virgindades; toma no sentido que quiseres.

**GREGORY.**
Têm de o tomar no sentido que o sentem.

**SAMPSON.**
Sentirão-no enquanto eu for capaz de ficar firme: e é sabido que sou um
belo pedaço de carne.

**GREGORY.**
Bem que não és peixe; se fosses, serias pobre João.
Desembainha a tua ferramenta; aqui vem da casa dos Montcchio.

es.title: Romeo y Julieta
es.synopsis: En la Verona tumultuosa, dos familias rivales, los Montesco y los Capuleto, viven en una animosidad perpetua. El destino, sin embargo, tiene otros planes para sus jóvenes herederos: Romeo, un apasionado soñador, y Julieta, una belleza de corazón indomable. Su primer encuentro es una chispa divina que ignora las barreras del odio familiar. A pesar de los celos ardientes, el rápido romance florece en secreto hasta que la tragedia se cierne sobre ellos. Forzados a enfrentar la ira de sus linajes y un destino implacable, Romeo y Julieta deben decidir: ¿vivir una vida fugaz llena de amor apasionado o sucumbir al veneno del odio? Esta es la historia eterna de dos almas predestinadas cuyo amor desafía el tiempo y la muerte.
es.fullText:
I. Un lugar público.
Escena II. Una calle.
Escena III. Habitación en la casa de Capuleto.
Escena IV. Una calle.
Escena V. Un salón en la casa de Capuleto.

**ACTO II**

CORO.

Escena I. Un lugar abierto adyacente al jardín de Capuleto.
Escena II. El jardín de Capuleto.
Escena III. La celda del Fraile Lorenzo.
Escena IV. Una calle.
Escena V. El jardín de Capuleto.
Escena VI. La celda del Fraile Lorenzo.

**ACTO III**

Escena I. Un lugar público.
Escena II. Una habitación en la casa de Capuleto.
Escena III. La celda del Fraile Lorenzo.
Escena IV. Una habitación en la casa de Capuleto.
Escena V. Una galería abierta hacia la cámara de Julieta, con vistas al jardín.

**ACTO IV**

Escena I. La celda del Fraile Lorenzo.
Escena II. Salón en la casa de Capuleto.
Escena III. La cámara de Julieta.
Escena IV. Salón en la casa de Capuleto.
Escena V. La cámara de Julieta; Julieta sobre la cama.

**ACTO V**

Escena I. Mântua. Una calle.
Escena II. La celda del Fraile Lorenzo.
Escena III. Un cementerio; en él un monumento perteneciente a los Capuleto.

***

### Personajes (*Dramatis Personae*)

**ESCALUS**, Príncipe de Verona.
**MERCUTIO**, pariente del Príncipe y amigo de Romeo.
**PARIS**, joven noble, pariente del Príncipe.
Paje de Paris.

**MONTAGUE**, cabeza de una familia veronesa en enemistad con los Capuleto.
**DAMA MONTAGUE**, esposa de Montague.
**ROMEO**, hijo de Montague.
**BENVOLIO**, sobrino de Montague y amigo de Romeo.
**ABRAM**, sirviente de Montague.
**BALTHASAR**, sirviente de Romeo.

**CAPULETO**, cabeza de una familia veronesa en enemistad con los Montague.
**DAMA CAPULETO**, esposa de Capuleto.
**JULIETA**, hija de Capuleto.
**TYBALT**, sobrino de Lady Capuleto.
**PRIMO DE CAPULETO**, un anciano.
**NIÑERA** de Julieta.
**PETER**, sirviente de la Niñera de Julieta.
**SAMPSON**, sirviente de Capuleto.
**GREGORY**, sirviente de Capuleto.
Sirvientes.

**FRAILE LORENZO**, franciscano.
**FRAILE JUAN**, de la misma Orden.
Un Boticario (*Apothecary*).
CORO.
Tres Músicos.
Un Oficial.
Ciudadanos de Verona; varios Hombres y Mujeres, parientes de ambas casas;
Enmascarados, Guardias, Vigilantes y Ayudantes.

**ESCENA.** Durante la mayor parte de la obra en Verona; una vez, en el Quinto Acto, en Mântua.

***

### PRÓLOGO

Entra Coro.

**CORO.**
Dos casas, ambas iguales en dignidad,
En hermosa Verona, donde situamos nuestra escena,
De antigua rencor rompen a nueva mutinería,
Donde la sangre civil mancha las manos civiles.
Desde las fatales entrañas de estos dos adversarios
Una pareja de amantes cruzados se quita la vida;
Cuyos padecibles e inesperados desenlaces
Entierran con su muerte la disputa de sus padres.
El temible tránsito del amor marcado por el destino,
Y la persistencia del furor de sus progenitores,
Que, solo con el fin de sus hijos, no podría remover,
Es ahora el transcurso de dos horas en nuestro escenario;
Lo cual, si atentas oídos prestáis,
Nuestra labor intentará remediar.

*[Sale.]*

***

**ACTO I**

**ESCENA I. Un lugar público.**

Entran Sampson y Gregory armados con espadas y escudos.

**SAMPSON.**
Gregory, por mi palabra, no llevaremos carbón.

**GREGORY.**
No, pues entonces seríamos acarreadores (*colliers*).

**SAMPSON.**
Quiero decir, si nos enojamos, sacaremos el acero.

**GREGORY.**
Sí, mientras vivas, sácate la garganta del collar.

**SAMPSON.**
Golpeo presto al sentirme conmovido.

**GREGORY.**
Mas no te mueves presto para golpear.

**SAMPSON.**
Un perro de la casa de Montague me mueve a actuar.

**GREGORY.**
Moverse es agitarse; y ser valiente es permanecer: por tanto, si te mueves, huyes.

**SAMPSON.**
Un perro de esa casa me moverá a quedarme firme. Tomaré el muro de cualquier hombre o doncella de Montague.

**GREGORY.**
Eso muestra que eres un esclavo débil, pues el más endeble va al muro.

**SAMPSON.**
Verdadero, y por ello las mujeres, siendo los recipientes más débiles, son siempre empujadas al muro: por tanto, yo empujaré a los hombres de Montague del muro, y arrojaré a sus doncellas contra él.

**GREGORY.**
La disputa es entre nuestros amos y nosotros sus hombres.

**SAMPSON.**
Es todo uno; me mostraré un tirano: cuando haya luchado con los hombres seré civil con las doncellas, les cortaré la cabeza.

**GREGORY.**
¿Las cabezas de las doncellas?

**SAMPSON.**
Sí, las cabezas de las doncellas, o su virginidad; tómalo en el sentido que quieras.

**GREGORY.**
Deben tomarlo en el sentido que lo sientan.

**SAMPSON.**
Ellas lo sentirán mientras pueda estar firme: y es sabido que soy una pieza de carne bastante fina.

**GREGORY.**
Bien hiciste no ser pez; si fueras, habrías sido pobre John. Saca tu herramienta; aquí viene de la casa de los Montagues.

Entran Abram y Balthasar.

**SAMPSON.**
Mi arma desnuda está lista: pelearemos, te respaldaré.

**GREGORY.**
¿Cómo? ¿Darle la espalda y correr?

**SAMPSON.**
No temas.

**GREGORY

fr.title: Roméo et Juliette
fr.synopsis: Dans la vibrante Verona, où les familles Montaigu et Capulet se livrent une guerre acharnée, Roméo, jeune passionné des Montaigu, rencontre Juliette, fille des Capulet, et tombe éperdument amoureux en un instant. Leur amour est une flamme fulgurante qui défie l'animosité ancestrale. Malgré les obstacles – le conflit de leurs maisons, les conventions sociales rigides, et la fatalité elle-même –, Roméo et Juliette s'engagent dans une romance clandestine, vibrant d'une intensité déchirante. Lorsque un malentendu tragique force les jeunes amants à sceller leur union par des vœux passionnés, le destin joue son jeu cruel. Après que Roméo ait été blessé mortellement, Juliette, persuadée de sa perte, se jette dans la mort. Le dénouement est déchirant : au réveil de Roméo, elle lui révèle avoir suivi son exemple. Leur amour sublime, mais trop puissant pour ce monde conflictuel, mène à une fin poignante où l'amour triomphe... juste assez longtemps pour être consumé par le drame.
fr.fullText:
***

**I.** Un lieu public.
**Scène II.** Une rue.
**Scène III.** Chambre dans la maison des Capulet.
**Scène IV.** Une rue.
**Scène V.** Galerie dans la maison des Capulet.

**ACTE II**

**CHŒUR.**
**Scène I.** Un lieu dégagé jouxtant le jardin des Capulet.
**Scène II.** Le jardin des Capulet.
**Scène III.** La cellule du Frère Lawrence.
**Scène IV.** Une rue.
**Scène V.** Le jardin des Capulet.
**Scène VI.** La cellule du Frère Lawrence.

***

**ACTE III**

**Scène I.** Un lieu public.
**Scène II.** Une chambre dans la maison des Capulet.
**Scène III.** La cellule du Frère Lawrence.
**Scène IV.** Une chambre dans la maison des Capulet.
**Scène V.** Une galerie ouverte donnant sur la chambre de Juliette, surplombant le jardin.

***

**ACTE IV**

**Scène I.** La cellule du Frère Lawrence.
**Scène II.** Galerie dans la maison des Capulet.
**Scène III.** Chambre de Juliette.
**Scène IV.** Galerie dans la maison des Capulet.
**Scène V.** Chambre de Juliette ; Juliette sur le lit.

***

**ACTE V**

**Scène I.** Mantoue. Une rue.
**Scène II.** La cellule du Frère Lawrence.
**Scène III.** Le cimetière ; dans celui-ci un monument appartenant aux Capulet.

***

### Personnages

**ESCALUS**, Prince de Vérone.
**MERCUTIO**, parent du Prince, et ami de Roméo.
**PARIS**, noble jeune homme, parent du Prince.
Page de Paris.

**MONTAGUE**, chef d'une famille véronais en querelle avec les Capulet.
**LADY MONTAGUE**, épouse de Montague.
**ROMÉO**, fils de Montague.
**BENVOLIO**, neveu de Montague, et ami de Roméo.
**ABRAM**, serviteur de Montague.
**BALTHASAR**, serviteur de Roméo.

**CAPULET**, chef d'une famille véronais en querelle avec les Montague.
**LADY CAPULET**, épouse de Capulet.
**JULIETTE**, fille de Capulet.
**TYBALT**, neveu de Lady Capulet.
**COUSIN DE CAPULET**, un vieil homme.
**NURSE (GOVERNANTE)** à Juliette.
**PETER**, serviteur de la Nurse.
**SAMPSON**, serviteur de Capulet.
**GREGORY**, serviteur de Capulet.
Serviteurs.

**FRÈRE LAWRENCE**, franciscain.
**FRÈRE JOHN**, du même Ordre.
Un Apothicaire.
**CHŒUR.**
Trois Musiciens.
Un Officier.
Citoyens de Vérone ; plusieurs Hommes et Femmes, parents des deux Maisons ;
Masques, Gardes, Veilleurs et Attendants.

***

**SCÈNE.** Durant la majeure partie de la pièce à Vérone ; une fois, dans le Cinquième Acte, à Mantoue.

***

### LE PRÉLUDE

*Entrée du Chœur.*

**CHŒUR.**
Deux maisons, toutes deux égales en dignité,
Dans belle Vérone, où nous mettons notre scène,
De vieille rancune brisent vers une nouvelle mutinerie,
Où le sang civique rend les mains civiles impures.
Des entrailles fatales de ces deux adversaires
Un couple d'amoureux croisés prend leur vie ;
Dont les malheureuses et pitoyables chutes
Enterrent avec leur mort la querelle de leurs parents.
Le passage effrayant de leur amour marqué par le destin,
Et la persistance de la rage de leurs parents,
Qui, sans l'issue de leurs enfants, rien ne peut lever,
Est maintenant le trafic de nos deux heures sur la scène ;
Ce qui, si vous avec oreilles patientes écoutez,
Notre labeur cherchera à réparer ce qui manque ici.

[*Sortie.*]

***

**ACTE I**

**SCÈNE I.** Un lieu public.

*Entrée de Sampson et Gregory armés de sabres et d'écussoirs.*

**SAMPSON.**
Gregory, sur ma parole, nous ne porterons pas de charbon.

**GREGORY.**
Non, car alors nous serions des charbonniers.

**SAMPSON.**
Je veux dire, si nous sommes en colère, nous tirerons l'épée.

**GREGORY.**
Oui, tant que tu vis, tire ton cou hors du collet.

**SAMPSON.**
Je frappe vite, quand je suis ému.

**GREGORY.**
Mais toi, tu ne t'émous pas vite à frapper.

**SAMPSON.**
Un chien de la maison Montague m'anime.

**GREGORY.**
Animer, c'est agiter ; et être vaillant, c'est tenir bon : donc, si tu es animé, tu fuis.

**SAMPSON.**
Un chien de cette maison m'anime à me tenir ferme. Je prendrai le mur de n'importe quel homme ou demoiselle des Montague.

**GREGORY.**
Cela montre un esclave faible, car le plus faible va au mur.

**SAMPSON.**
Vrai, et donc les femmes, étant les vaisseaux les plus faibles, sont toujours poussées contre le mur : donc je pousserai les hommes de Montague du mur, et j'enfoncerai ses demoiselles dans le mur.

**GREGORY.**
La querelle est entre nos maîtres et nous leurs hommes.

**SAMPSON.**
C'est tout unie ; je vais me montrer tyran : quand j'aurai combattu les hommes, je serai civil avec les demoiselles, je leur couperai la tête.

**GREGORY.**
Les têtes des demoiselles ?

**SAMPSON.**
Oui, les têtes des demoiselles, ou leurs virginités ; prends cela comme tu voudras.

**GREGORY.**
Ils devront le prendre dans le sens qu'ils ressentent.

**SAMPSON.**
Ils le sentiront pendant que je suis capable de tenir : et il est connu que je suis une belle pièce de chair.

**GREGORY.**
Il est bien que tu ne sois pas un poisson ; si tu l'étais, tu serais Poor John. Tire ton outil ; voici des gens de la maison des Montagues.

*Entrée d'Abram et Balthasar.*
