es.title: Las Aventuras de Sherlock Holmes
es.synopsis: En el bullicioso Londres victoriano, un genio deductivo con una mente tan aguda como su pipa se alza para desentrañar los misterios más enigmáticos de la ciudad. Junto a su leal compañero, el Dr. Watson, Sherlock Holmes navega por intrigas criminales que desafían toda lógica aparente. Desde robos inexplicables y desapariciones fantasmales hasta crímenes meticulosamente orquestados, Holmes aplica su método científico: observar, analizar y deducir. Acompáñenos en esta serie de aventuras donde la verdad siempre esconde una capa más de misterio, demostrando que detrás del velo de lo inexplicable, late el pulso frío e infalible de la razón.
es.fullText:
# I. Un Escándalo en Bohemia

## II. La Liga del Pelirojo
## III. Un Caso de Identidad
## IV. El Misterio del Valle de Boscombe
## V. Los Cinco Pips Naranjas
## VI. El Hombre de los Labios Torcidos
## VII. La Aventura del Carbunclo Azul
## VIII. La Aventura de la Banda Manchada
## IX. La Aventura del Pulgar del Ingeniero
## X. La Aventura del Soltero Noble
## XI. La Aventura del Coronel de Berilo
## XII. La Aventura de los Fresnos de Cobre

***

# I. UN ESCÁNDALO EN BOHEMIA

I.

Para Sherlock Holmes ella es siempre *la* mujer. Rara vez le he oído mencionarla bajo otro nombre. En sus ojos eclipsa y predomina sobre todo su sexo. No era que sintiera alguna emoción semejante al amor por Irene Adler; todas las emociones, y esa en particular, eran abominables para su mente fría, precisa pero admirablemente equilibrada. Él era, supongo, la máquina de razonamiento y observación más perfecta que ha visto el mundo, pero como amante se habría puesto en una posición falaz. Jamás hablaba de las pasiones más suaves, salvo con un gesto burlón y un desdén. Eran cosas admirables para el observador —excelentes para levantar el velo de los motivos y acciones de los hombres—. Pero que el razonador entrenado admitiera tales intrusiones en su delicado y finamente ajustado temperamento era introducir un factor distractor capaz de hacer dudar de todos sus resultados mentales. Una mota de polvo en un instrumento sensible, o una grieta en uno de sus propios lentes de alta potencia, no sería más perturbadora que una fuerte emoción en una naturaleza como la suya. Y, sin embargo, solo había una mujer para él, y esa mujer era la difunta Irene Adler, de memoria dudosa y cuestionable.

Había visto poco de Holmes últimamente. Mi matrimonio nos había distanciado. Mi propia felicidad completa, e intereses centrados en el hogar que surgen alrededor del hombre que por primera vez se siente amo de su propio establecimiento, eran suficientes para absorber toda mi atención, mientras Holmes, quien detestaba cualquier forma de sociedad con toda su alma bohemia, permanecía en nuestro alojamiento en Baker Street, enterrado entre sus viejos libros, y alternando de semana en semana entre la cocaína y la ambición, el somnolio del narcótico y la feroz energía de su propia aguda naturaleza. Todavía estaba profundamente atraído por el estudio del crimen, ocupando sus inmensas facultades y extraordinarios poderes de observación siguiendo aquellos indicios, y desentrañando aquellos misterios que habían sido abandonados como desesperados por la policía oficial.

De vez en cuando escuchaba algún relato vago de sus gestiones: de su citación a Odesa en el caso del asesinato de Trepoff, de su esclarecimiento de la singular tragedia de los hermanos Atkinson en Trincomalee, y finalmente de la misión que había cumplido con tanta delicadeza y éxito para la familia reinante de Holanda. Sin embargo, más allá de estas señales de su actividad —que meramente compartía con todos los lectores de la prensa diaria— sabía poco de mi antiguo amigo y compañero.

Una noche —fue el veinte de marzo de 1888— regresaba de un viaje a un paciente (pues ya había vuelto a la práctica civil), cuando mi camino me llevó por Baker Street. Al pasar junto a la bien recordada puerta, que siempre debe asociarse en mi mente con mi cortejo y con los oscuros sucesos del *Estudio en Escarlata*, fui invadida por un intenso deseo de volver a ver a Holmes y saber cómo estaba empleando sus extraordinarios poderes. Sus aposentos estaban brillantemente iluminados y, justo cuando levanté la vista, vi su alta y esbelta figura pasar dos veces en una oscura silueta contra el visillo. Estaba paseándose por la habitación con rapidez, ansiosamente, con la cabeza hundida sobre el pecho y las manos entrelazadas a la espalda. Para mí, que conocía cada uno de sus humores y hábitos, su actitud y porte contaban su propia historia. Estaba trabajando de nuevo. Había emergido de los sueños creados por sus drogas y estaba ávido del rastro de algún nuevo problema. Hice sonar el timbre y me hicieron subir a la cámara que antes era en parte mía.

Su semblante no fue efusivo. Rara vez lo era; pero estaba contento, creo, de verme. Con apenas una palabra dicha, sino con un ojo amable, me hizo señas hacia un sillón, arrojó su estuche de cigarros y señaló un recipiente para licores y una gasogena en la esquina. Luego se paró frente a la chimenea y me examinó con su singular aire introspectivo.

"Wedlock le sienta bien", comentó. "Creo, Watson, que has engordado siete libras y media desde que te vi".

"¡Siete!", respondí.

"En efecto, debería haber pensado un poco más. Solo un poquito más, me parece, Watson. Y de nuevo en la práctica, observo. No me dijiste que tenías intención de volver al trabajo."

"Entonces, ¿cómo lo sabes?"

"Lo veo, lo deduzco. ¿Cómo sé que últimamente te has estado 'mojando' mucho, y que tienes una doncella sirvienta muy torpe y descuidada?"

"Mi querido Holmes", dije, "esto es demasiado. Seguramente habrías sido quemado si vivieras unos siglos atrás. Es cierto que hice una caminata por el campo el jueves y volví en un terrible desorden, pero como he cambiado de ropa no puedo imaginar cómo lo deduces. En cuanto a Mary Jane, es incorregible, y mi esposa le ha dado preaviso, pero ahí, de nuevo, no logro ver cómo lo haces."

Él se rió para sí mismo y frotó sus largas y nerviosas manos.

"Es pura sencillez", dijo; "mis ojos me dicen que en el interior de tu zapato izquierdo, justo donde la luz del fuego lo golpea, el cuero está surcado por seis cortes casi paralelos. Obviamente han sido causados por alguien que ha raspado con gran descuido los bordes de la suela para quitarle barro incrustado. Por consiguiente, ves, mi doble deducción de que has estado fuera en un clima vil y que tienes una pieza particularmente maligna del *slavy* londinense. En cuanto a tu práctica, si un caballero entra en mis aposentos oliendo a yodoformo, con una marca negra de nitrato de plata sobre su dedo anular derecho, y una protuberancia en el lado derecho de su sombrero de copa para mostrar dónde ha escondido su estetoscopio, debo ser tosco, ciertamente, si no lo declaro

fr.title: Les Aventures de Sherlock Holmes
fr.synopsis: Dans le Londres victorien, où les ombres des antiquités recèlent plus de mystères que les recoins les plus sombres, Sherlock Holmes, détective hors pair, est appelé à résoudre une série d'énigmes insolubles. Sous la houlette perspicace du Dr Watson, il plonge dans les intrigues complexes de son époque. De l'étrange disparition au crime prémédité, chaque cas révèle une facette nouvelle de l'ingéniosité analytique de Holmes. Accompagné de ses célèbres méthodes – observation minutieuse, déduction infaillible et jeu d'esprit aiguisé –, il défie les criminels les plus rusés. Aux côtés de Dr Watson, le lecteur est entraîné dans une odyssée fascinante où la vérité n'est jamais loin, mais toujours soigneusement dissimulée derrière un voile de secrets palpitants.

***

# I. UN SCANDALE EN BOHÈME

### I.

Pour Sherlock Holmes, elle est *toujours* la femme. J'ai rarement entendu le mentionner sous un autre nom. Dans ses yeux, elle éclipse et domine tout son sexe. Ce n'était pas qu'il ressentît une émotion qui s'apparentait à l'amour pour Irene Adler. Toutes les émotions, et celle-là en particulier, étaient abhorrantes à son esprit froid, précis mais admirablement équilibré. Il était, je le crois, la machine de raisonnement et d'observation la plus parfaite que le monde ait jamais vue, mais en tant qu'amant, il s'est placé dans une position fausse. Il ne parlait jamais des passions les plus douces, sauf avec une moquerie et un ricanement. Elles étaient admirables pour l'observateur — excellentes pour lever le voile sur les motifs et les actions des hommes. Mais pour le raisonneur aguerri d'admettre de telles intrusions dans son propre tempérament délicat et finement ajusté, c'était introduire un facteur de distraction susceptible de jeter le doute sur tous ses résultats mentaux. Du sable dans un instrument sensible, ou une fissure dans l'un de ses objectifs à haute puissance, ne serait pas plus perturbant qu'une forte émotion chez une nature telle que la sienne. Et pourtant, il n'y avait qu'une seule femme pour lui, et cette femme était Irene Adler, dotée d'une mémoire douteuse et sujette à questionnement.

J'avais peu vu Holmes récemment. Mon mariage nous avait éloignés l'un de l'autre. Mon propre bonheur absolu, ainsi que les intérêts centrés sur le foyer qui se dressent autour de celui qui se trouve d'abord maître de son propre établissement, étaient suffisants pour absorber toute mon attention, tandis que Holmes, qui détestait toute forme de société avec son âme bohème entière, demeurait dans notre logement de Baker Street, enseveli parmi ses vieux livres, et alternant semaine après semaine entre la cocaïne et l'ambition — la somnolence du narcotique et l'énergie féroce de sa propre nature perspicace. Il était toujours, comme à jamais, profondément attiré par l'étude du crime, occupant ses immenses facultés et son extraordinaire pouvoir d'observation en suivant les indices et en élucidant ces mystères que la police officielle avait abandonnés comme désespérés.

De temps à autre, j'entendais un récit vague de ses agissements : de son convocation à Odessa dans l'affaire du meurtre Trepoff, de son élucidation de la tragédie singulière des frères Atkinson à Trincomalee, et enfin de la mission qu'il avait accomplie avec tant de délicatesse et de succès pour la famille régnante des Pays-Bas. Au-delà de ces signes de son activité, que je partageais simplement avec tous les lecteurs du journal quotidien, j'en savais peu de mon ancien ami et compagnon.

Une nuit — c'était le vingt mars 1888 — je revenais d'un déplacement auprès d'un patient (car j'étais revenu à la pratique civile), lorsque mon chemin me mena par Baker Street. En passant devant la porte bien mémorisée, qui doit toujours être associée dans mon esprit à ma cour et aux incidents sombres du *Study in Scarlet*, je fus saisi d'un vif désir de revoir Holmes, et de savoir comment il employait ses pouvoirs extraordinaires. Ses pièces étaient brillamment éclairées, et même en levant les yeux, je vis sa silhouette haute et dégingandée passer deux fois dans une ombre sombre contre le rideau. Il faisait les cent pas dans la pièce, rapidement, avec avidité, la tête baissée sur sa poitrine et les mains jointes derrière lui. Pour moi, qui connais tous ses humeurs et habitudes, son attitude et sa manière racontaient leur propre histoire. Il était de nouveau au travail. Il s'était élevé de ses rêves créés par le narcotique et était pris dans la traque d'un nouveau problème. J'ai sonné à la porte et on m'a fait monter dans la chambre qui avait autrefois été en partie la mienne.

Sa manière n'était pas exubérante. Elle l'est rarement ; mais il était ravi, je pense, de me voir. Sans même prononcer un mot, mais avec un regard bienveillant, il m'a fait signe d'aller vers un fauteuil, a jeté sur son étui de cigares et indiqué une boîte à spiritueux et une gasogène dans le coin. Puis il s'est tenu devant la cheminée et m'a examiné dans sa singulière manière introspective.

« Le mariage te va bien, » a-t-il fait remarquer. « Je pense, Watson, que tu as pris sept livres et demi depuis que je t'ai vu. »

« Sept ! » ai-je répondu.

« En effet, j'aurais dû penser un peu plus. Juste une petite touche de plus, je crois, Watson. Et à nouveau en pratique, observe. Tu ne m'as pas dit que tu allais prendre le métier. »

« Alors, comment le savez-vous ? »

« Je le vois, je le déduis. Comment puis-je savoir que tu te rends très mou récemment, et que tu as une servante particulièrement maladroite et négligente ? »

« Mon cher Holmes, » ai-je dit, « c'est trop. Vous auriez certainement été brûlé, si vous viviez quelques siècles auparavant. C'est vrai que j'ai fait une promenade à la campagne jeudi et suis rentré dans un état affreux, mais comme j'ai changé de vêtements, je ne peux imaginer comment vous le déduisez. Quant à Mary Jane, elle est incorrigible, et ma femme lui a donné son préavis, mais là encore, j'arrive pas à comprendre votre raisonnement. »

Il pouffa de rire en lui-même et frotta ses longues mains nerveuses ensemble.

« C'est une simplicité même, » dit-il ; « mes yeux me disent que sur l'intérieur de ta chaussure gauche, juste là où la lumière du feu la frappe, le cuir est

title: A Ilha do Tesouro
author: R.L. Stevenson
synopsis: Em meio ao vasto azul, jaz uma ilha envolta em lendas e riquezas incalculáveis. Jovem e audacioso Jim Hawkins se vê arrastado para o coração de um mistério marítimo quando seu navio encontra o infame Capitão Flint – mestre dos tesouros perdidos e senhor de um apetite voraz por aventura. Entre tempestades traiçoeiras, mapas enigmáticos e a sombra ameaçadora de piratas sedentos por ouro, Jim embarca em uma caçada frenética. Ele será capaz de desvendar os segredos da ilha antes que o tesouro seja tomado pela ganância, ou pior: tragado pelo mar? Prepare-se para navegar nas águas turbulentas onde a coragem e a traição definem quem sobreviverá ao saque!

# ILHA DO TESOURO

### PARTE UM — O Velho Bucaneiro

**I**
O velho marinheiro no Admiral Benbow

*Squire Trelawney, Dr. Livesey e os demais cavalheiros,* tendo me pedido que registrasse todos os pormenores sobre a Ilha do Tesouro, do começo ao fim, sem omitir nada — senão as coordenadas da ilha, apenas porque ainda há tesouro por levantar —, tomo minha pena no ano de graça 17—, e volto ao tempo em que meu pai administrava a estalagem Admiral Benbow e o velho marinheiro marrom, com seu sabre cortado, tomou sua acomodação sob nosso teto.

Lembro-o como se fosse ontem, quando ele vinha caminhando até a porta da estalagem, seu baú de mar seguindo atrás em um carrinho de mão — um homem alto, forte, pesado e cor de noz, com uma trança castanha caída sobre o ombro do casaco azul sujo, com mãos ásperas e marcadas, com unhas pretas e quebradiças, e o sabre cortado atravessando uma bochecha, de um branco sujo e lív

en.title: Treasure Island
en.synopsis: Young Jim Hawkins stumbles upon a map leading to a legendary pirate hoard, igniting an adventure that soon plunges him into the lawless grandeur of the high seas. Alongside grizzled sailors and cunning buccaneers, he encounters the formidable Captain Flint's ghost—and the terrifying reality embodied by the monstrous Long John Silver. As their ship sails toward hidden shores, treachery looms like a storm cloud; loyalties are tested, mutiny brews beneath the tropical sun, and Jim must navigate a treacherous web of deceit to survive the pursuit of gold. *Treasure Island* is a vibrant tapestry of adventure, where youthful innocence confronts savage ambition in a glittering contest for buried riches.

TREASURE ISLAND




PART ONE--The Old Buccaneer




I
The Old Sea-dog at the Admiral Benbow


Squire Trelawney, Dr. Livesey, and the rest of these gentlemen having
asked me to write down the whole particulars about Treasure Island, from
the beginning to the end, keeping nothing back but the bearings of the
island, and that only because there is still treasure not yet lifted, I
take up my pen in the year of grace 17—, and go back to the time when
my father kept the Admiral Benbow inn and the brown old seaman with the
sabre cut first took up his lodging under our roof.

I remember him as if it were yesterday, as he came plodding to the
inn door, his sea-chest following behind him in a hand-barrow--a
tall, strong, heavy, nut-brown man, his tarry pigtail falling over the
shoulder of his soiled blue coat, his hands ragged and scarred, with
black, broken nails, and the sabre cut across one cheek, a dirty, livid
white. I remember him looking round the cove and whistling to himself
as he did so, and then breaking out in that old sea-song that he sang so
often afterwards:

          “Fifteen men on the dead man’s chest--
             Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum!”

in the high, old tottering voice that seemed to have been tuned and
broken at the capstan bars. Then he rapped on the door with a bit of
stick like a handspike that he carried, and when my father appeared,
called roughly for a glass of rum. This, when it was brought to him,
he drank slowly, like a connoisseur, lingering on the taste and still
looking about him at the cliffs and up at our signboard.

“This is a handy cove,” says he at length; “and a pleasant sittyated
grog-shop. Much company, mate?”

My father told him no, very little company, the more was the pity.

“Well, then,” said he, “this is the berth for me. Here you, matey,” he
cried to the man who trundled the barrow; “bring up alongside and help
up my chest. I’ll stay here a bit,” he continued. “I’m a plain man; rum
and bacon and eggs is what I want, and that head up there for to watch
ships off. What you mought call me? You mought call me captain. Oh, I
see what you’re at--there”; and he threw down three or four gold pieces
on the threshold. “You can tell me when I’ve worked through that,” says
he, looking as fierce as a commander.

And indeed bad as his clothes were and coarsely as he spoke, he had none
of the appearance of a man who sailed before the mast, but seemed like
a mate or skipper accustomed to be obeyed or to strike. The man who came
with the barrow told us the mail had set him down the morning before at
the Royal George, that he had inquired what inns there were along the
coast, and hearing ours well spoken of, I suppose, and described as
lonely, had chosen it from the others for his place of residence. And
that was all we could learn of our guest.

He was a very silent man by custom. All day he hung round the cove or
upon the cliffs with a brass telescope; all evening he sat in a corner
of the parlour next the fire and drank rum and water very strong. Mostly
he would not speak when spoken to, only look up sudden and fierce and
blow through his nose like a fog-horn; and we and the people who came
about our house soon learned to let him be. Every day when he came back
from his stroll he would ask if any seafaring men had gone by along the
road. At first we thought it was the want of company of his own kind
that made him ask this question, but at last we began to see he was
desirous to avoid them. When a seaman did put up at the Admiral Benbow
(as now and then some did, making by the coast road for Bristol) he
would look in at him through the curtained door before he entered the
parlour; and he was always sure to be as silent as a mouse when any such
was present. For me, at least, there was no secret about the matter, for
I was, in a way, a sharer in his alarms. He had taken me aside one day
and promised me a silver fourpenny on the first of every month if I
would only keep my “weather-eye open for a seafaring man with one leg”
 and let him know the moment he appeared. Often enough when the first
of the month came round and I applied to him for my wage, he would only
blow through his nose at me and stare me down, but before the week was
out he was sure to think better of it, bring me my four-penny piece, and
repeat his orders to look out for “the seafaring man with one leg.”

How that personage haunted my dreams, I need scarcely tell you. On
stormy nights, when the wind shook the four corners of the house and
the surf roared along the cove and up the cliffs, I would see him in a
thousand forms, and with a thousand diabolical expressions. Now the leg
would be cut off at the knee, now at the hip; now he was a monstrous
kind of a creature who had never had but the one leg, and that in the
middle of his body. To see him leap and run and pursue me over hedge and
ditch was the worst of nightmares. And altogether I paid pretty dear for
my monthly fourpenny piece, in the shape of these abominable fancies.

But though I was so terrified by the idea of the seafaring man with one
leg, I was far less afraid of the captain himself than anybody else who
knew him. There were nights when he took a deal more rum and water
than his head would carry; and then he would sometimes sit and sing his
wicked, old, wild sea-songs, minding nobody; but sometimes he would call
for glasses round and force all the trembling company to listen to his
stories or bear a chorus to his singing. Often I have heard the house
shaking with “Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum,” all the neighbours joining
in for dear life, with the fear of death upon them, and each singing
louder than the other to avoid remark. For in these fits he was the most
overriding companion ever known; he would slap his hand on the table for
silence all round; he would fly up in a passion of anger at a question,
or sometimes because none was put, and so he judged the company was not
following his story. Nor would he allow anyone to leave the inn till he
had drunk himself sleepy and reeled off to bed.

His stories were what frightened people worst of all. Dreadful stories
they were--about hanging, and walking the plank, and storms at sea, and
the Dry Tortugas, and wild deeds and places on the Spanish Main. By his
own account he must have lived his life among some of the wickedest men
that God ever allowed upon the sea, and the language in which he told
these stories shocked our plain country people almost as much as the
crimes that he described. My father was always saying the inn would be
ruined, for people would soon cease coming there to be tyrannized over
and put down, and sent shivering to their beds; but I really believe his
presence did us good. People were frightened at the time, but on looking
back they rather liked it; it was a fine excitement in a quiet country
life, and there was even a party of the younger men who pretended to
admire him, calling him a “true sea-dog” and a “real old salt” and
such like names, and saying there was the sort of man that made England
terrible at sea.

In one way, indeed, he bade fair to ruin us, for he kept on staying week
after week, and at last month after month, so that all the money had
been long exhausted, and still my father never plucked up the heart to
insist on having more. If ever he mentioned it, the captain blew through
his nose so loudly that you might say he roared, and stared my poor
father out of the room. I have seen him wringing his hands after such a
rebuff, and I am sure the annoyance and the terror he lived in must have
greatly hastened his early and unhappy death.

All the time he lived with us the captain made no change whatever in his
dress but to buy some stockings from a hawker. One of the cocks of his
hat having fallen down, he let it hang from that day forth, though it
was a great annoyance when it blew. I remember the appearance of his
coat, which he patched himself upstairs in his room, and which, before
the end, was nothing but patches. He never wrote or received a letter,
and he never spoke with any but the neighbours, and with these, for the
most part, only when drunk on rum. The great sea-chest none of us had
ever seen open.

He was only once crossed, and that was towards the end, when my poor
father was far gone in a decline that took him off. Dr. Livesey came
late one afternoon to see the patient, took a bit of dinner from my
mother, and went into the parlour to smoke a pipe until his horse should
come down from the hamlet, for we had no stabling at the old Benbow. I
followed him in, and I remember observing the contrast the neat, bright
doctor, with his powder as white as snow and his bright, black eyes and
pleasant manners, made with the coltish country folk, and above all,
with that filthy, heavy, bleared scarecrow of a pirate of ours, sitting,
far gone in rum, with his arms on the table. Suddenly he--the captain,
that is--began to pipe up his eternal song:

          “Fifteen men on the dead man’s chest--
             Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum!
           Drink and the devil had done for the rest--
             Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum!”

At first I had supposed “the dead man’s chest” to be that identical big
box of his upstairs in the front room, and the thought had been mingled
in my nightmares with that of the one-legged se

### PARTE UM — O Velho Bucaneiro

O velho marinheiro no Admiral Benbow

pt-pt.title: A Ilha do Tesouro
pt-pt.synopsis: Uma herança misteriosa, um mapa enigmático e uma ilha recheada de perigos aguardam o jovem Jim Hawkins. Ao descobrir vestígios de tesouros perdidos, ele arrasta para o coração do mar a bordo do *Hispaniola*, onde se cruza com piratas sanguinários e capitães astutos. Entre tempestades bravias, encontros mortais e segredos ancestrais, Jim mergulha numa aventura épica: uma caçada feroz ao ouro que pode selar seu destino entre a glória de um corsário ou o terror de um naufrágio sangrento.
pt-pt.fullText:
# TREASURE ISLAND

**PARTE UM – O VELHO PIRATA**

***

### I. O Velho Marujo no Admiral Benbow

Squire Trelawney, Dr. Livesey e o resto destes cavalheiros pediram-me que registasse todo o detalhe acerca da Ilha do Tesouro, desde o princípio até ao fim, sem omitir nada — senão a posição geográfica da ilha, apenas porque ainda há tesouros por levantar —, tomo a minha pena no ano de graça 17—, e retrocedo no tempo até à época em que meu pai mantinha a estalagem Admiral Benbow e o marujo castanho, com o sabre na mão, tomou lugar de residência sob o nosso teto.

Lembro-me dele como se fosse ontem, quando ele vinha cambaleando até a porta da estalagem, o seu baú de marujo seguindo-o num carrinho de mão — um homem alto, forte e pesado, cor de noz castanho, com uma trança de tarra caindo sobre o ombro do seu casaco azul sujo; as suas mãos eram grosseiras e marcadas por cicatrizes, com unhas pretas e quebradas, e o sabre cortava uma das maçãs do rosto, deixando-a num branco sujo e lívido. Lembro-me dele a olhar à volta da enseada e a assobiar para si próprio enquanto o fazia, e depois irromper naquele velho cântico marítimo que cantava com tanta frequência depois:

> “Quinze homens no peito do morto —
> *Yo-ho-ho*, e uma garrafa de rum!”

Numa voz aguda, velha e vacilante, como se tivesse sido afinada e partida nos mestres-eixos. Então ele bateu à porta com um pedaço de pau, tal como um espigão que carregava, e quando meu pai apareceu, gritou rudemente por uma taça de rum. Isto, quando lhe foi trazido, bebeu devagar, como um conhecedor, demorando-se no sabor e ainda a olhar à sua volta para os penhascos e para o nosso letreiro.

“Esta é uma enseada conveniente”, disse ele finalmente; “e uma estalagem de *grog* agradável. Muita companhia, companheiro?”

Meu pai informou-lhe que não, pouca companhia, o que era pena.

“Bem, então”, disse ele, “este é o meu lugar. Aqui está tu, marinheiro”, gritou para o homem que empurrava o carrinho; “traz junto e ajuda a subir o meu baú. Ficarei aqui um bocado”, continuou. “Sou um homem simples; rum com bacon e ovos é o que quero, e aquela cabeça lá em cima para vigiar os navios à distância. O que me chamais? Podes chamar-me capitão. Oh, já percebi a tua intenção — ali”; e atirou três ou quatro moedas de ouro no limiar. “Poderás dizer-me quando tiver feito o meu trabalho”, disse ele, com um olhar tão feroz quanto o de um comandante.

E de facto, por mais que as suas roupas fossem más e a sua fala grosseira, não tinha a aparência de um homem que navegasse à frente do mastro; parecia ser um marinheiro ou capitão habituado a ser obedecido ou a dar ordem. O homem que veio com o carrinho contou-nos que os correios o tinham deixado na véspera no Royal George, e que ele havia perguntado por estalagens ao longo da costa, e ouvindo falar bem das nossas, suponho, e descrevendo-as como solitárias, escolhera-a entre as outras para a sua morada. E era tudo o que podíamos saber sobre o nosso hóspede.

Ele era um homem muito silencioso por natureza. O dia todo passeava pela enseada ou pelos penhascos com um telescópio de latão; toda a noite sentava-se num canto da sala ao lado da lareira e bebia rum e água bem fortes. Na maioria das vezes, não falava quando lhe era dirigido a palavra, apenas levantava o olhar de repente e ferozmente e bufava pelo nariz como um apito de nevoeiro; e nós, junto com as pessoas que vinham à nossa casa, aprendemos rapidamente a deixá-lo estar. Todos os dias, ao regressar do seu passeio, perguntava se algum marinheiro tinha passado pela estrada. No início pensámos que fosse falta de companhia da sua espécie o que o levava a fazer essa pergunta, mas acabamos por perceber que ele desejava evitá-los. Quando um marinheiro parava no Admiral Benbow (como ocasionalmente acontecia, vindo pela estrada costeira em direção a Bristol), ele olhava para ele através da porta coberta antes de entrar na sala; e estava sempre certo em ser tão silencioso quanto um rato quando algum assim estava presente. Para mim, pelo menos, não havia segredo no assunto, pois eu era, de certa forma, partilheiro dos seus alvoroços. Ele tinha-me chamado a parte um dia e prometido-me uma moeda de quatro peniques de prata no primeiro de cada mês se apenas mantivesse "o olho do tempo atento para um marinheiro com uma perna" e o avisasse no momento em que ele aparecesse. Frequentemente, quando chegava o primeiro do mês e lhe pedia o meu salário, ele apenas bufava pelo nariz em minha direção e fixava-me com o olhar, mas antes de a semana terminar era certo que mudava de ideia, trazia-me a minha moeda de quatro peniques e repetia as suas ordens de vigiar "o marinheiro com uma perna".

Como essa figura assombrava os meus sonhos, mal preciso dizer-te. Nas noites tempestuosas, quando o vento sacudia os quatro cantos da casa e o mar bravio rugia pela enseada e pelos penhascos, eu via-o em mil formas, e com mil expressões diabólicas. Por vezes a perna era cortada ao joelho, noutras à anca; por vezes ele era uma espécie monstruosa de criatura que nunca tinha tido mais do que uma perna, e esta no meio do seu corpo. Ver-lhe saltar, correr e perseguir-me sobre sebes e valas era o pior dos pesadelos. E em suma, pagava um preço considerável pelo meu pedaço mensal de quatro peniques, na forma destas abomináveis fantasias.

Mas embora estivesse tão aterrorizado com a ideia do marinheiro com uma perna, estava muito menos apavorado do capitão em si do que qualquer outra pessoa que o conhecesse. Havia noites em que ele consumia mais rum e água do que a sua cabeça aguentava; e então às vezes sentava-se e cantava os seus cantos marítimos maus, antigos

es.title: La Isla del Tesoro
es.synopsis: Bajo el sol inclemente, donde las olas susurran secretos ancestrales, se alza la misteriosa Isla del Tesoro. Un mapa amarillento y polvoriento promete riquezas inimaginables, atrayendo a Jim Hawkins y a su audaz tripulación en una carrera por el oro perdido. Pero esta aventura no es solo sobre cofres brillantes; es un torbellino de intriga marina donde la codicia se enfrenta al peligro mortal. Entre piratas feroces, traiciones bajo el velamen y monstruosas bestias marinas, ¿lograrán los héroes alcanzar la X marcada en el mapa? Descubre si la promesa del tesoro vale el precio de las vidas que se arriesgan en esa idílica, pero letal, tierra isleña.

# EL BARCO DEL TESORO

### PARTE UNO—El Viejo Bucanero

#### **I**
**El Viejo Marino en el Admiral Benbow**

Squire Trelawney, Dr. Livesey y el resto de estos caballeros habiéndome pedido que anotara todos los detalles sobre la Isla del Tesoro, desde el principio hasta el final, sin omitir nada más que las coordenadas de la isla—y solo porque todavía hay tesoros sin desenterrar—tomo mi pluma en el año de gracia 17\_\_, y vuelvo al tiempo en que mi padre regentaba la posada Admiral Benbow y el marrón viejo marinero, con su sable en mano, tomó primero alojamiento bajo nuestro techo.

Lo recuerdo como si fuera ayer, cuando se acercó arrastrando los pies a la puerta de la posada; su cofre marítimo lo seguía en un carro de manos—un hombre alto, fuerte, pesado y de color castaño nuez, con su coleta tiznada cayéndole sobre el hombro de su gastado abrigo azul, sus manos ásperas y cicatrizadas, con uñas negras y rotas, y el sable cortándole una mejilla, un blanco sucio y lividecísimo. Lo recuerdo mirando alrededor de la bahía y silbando para sí mismo mientras lo hacía, y luego estallando en aquella vieja canción marinera que cantaba tan a menudo después:

> *"Quince hombres sobre el pecho del muerto—*
> *¡Yo-ho-ho, y una botella de ron!"*

...en aquella voz alta, antigua y tambaleante que parecía haber sido afinada y quebrada en las barras del cabrestante. Luego golpeó

fr.title: L'Île au Trésor
fr.synopsis: Sur une île lointaine, rongée par le mystère et les légendes d'un trésor enfoui, se déroule une aventure palpitante. Le jeune Jim Hawkins, témoin de la rencontre entre des marins excentriques – un capitaine charismatique mais imprévisible et son premier officier taciturne – se retrouve plongé au cœur du tumulte. Entre tempêtes soudaines, chasse aux pirates redoutables et découvertes épiques, Jim est forcé d'affronter le danger et la cupidité. Le trésor promis n'est pas seulement une montagne d'or ; c'est aussi une épreuve pour son courage. Alors que l'ombre du capitaine Flint plane sur les récifs, comment ce jeune garçon survivra-t-il à la fureur des mers pour révéler le secret de cette île maudite ?
fr.fullText:
# *Treasure Island*

**PART ONE—The Old Buccaneer**

***

## I. The Old Sea-dog at the Admiral Benbow . . . . 11

Squire Trelawney, Dr. Livesey, and the rest of these gentlemen having asked me to set down all the particulars concerning Treasure Island, from its very beginning unto its end—keeping nothing back save the bearings of the island, and that only because there remains still treasure yet unlifted—I take up my pen in the year of grace 17—, and return to the time when my father kept the Admiral Benbow inn, and the brown old seaman with the sabre cut first took his lodging under our roof.

I remember him as if it were but yesterday, as he came trudging toward the inn door, his sea-chest following behind him in a hand-barrow—a tall, stout, heavy man, of nut-brown complexion; his tarry pigtail fell over the shoulder of his soiled blue coat; his hands were ragged and scarred, with black, broken nails, and the sabre cut across one cheek, a dirty, livid white. I recall him looking around the cove and whistling to himself as he did so, and then breaking into that old sea-song which he sang so often afterwards:

> “Fifteen men on the dead man’s chest—
> Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum!”

in the high, aged voice that seemed tuned and broken upon the capstan bars. Then he rapped upon the door with a bit of stick, like a handspike that he carried, and when my father appeared, called roughly for a glass of rum. This, when brought to him, he drank slowly, like a connoisseur, lingering upon the taste whilst still looking about at the cliffs and up at our signboard.

“This is a handy cove,” says he at last; “and a pleasant sittyated grog-shop. Much company, mate?”

My father told him no, very little company, the more was the pity.

“Well, then,” said he, “this is the berth for me. Here you, matey,” he cried to the man who trundled the barrow; “bring up alongside and help up my chest. I’ll stay here a bit,” he continued. “I am a plain man; rum and bacon and eggs is what I want, and that head up there for to watch ships off. What you mought call me? You mought call me captain. Oh, I see what you’re at—there”; and he threw down three or four gold pieces upon the threshold. “You can tell me when I’ve worked through that,” says he, looking as fierce as a commander.

And indeed, though poor were his clothes and coarse his speech, he possessed none of the appearance of a man who sailed before the mast; rather, he resembled a mate or skipper accustomed to being obeyed or to striking. The man who accompanied him with the barrow told us that the mail had set him down the morning prior at the Royal George, that he had inquired after inns along the coast, and hearing ours well spoken of—I suppose, and described as lonely—he had chosen it from the others for his place of residence. And that was all we could learn of our guest.

He was a very silent man by custom. All day he lingered around the cove or upon the cliffs with a brass telescope; all evening he sat in a corner of the parlour next to the fire and drank rum and water very strong. Mostly, he would not speak when spoken to, but only look up—sudden and fierce—and blow through his nose like a fog-horn; and we, along with the people who frequented our house, soon learned to let him be. Every day, upon returning from his stroll, he would inquire if any seafaring men had passed by along the road. At first, we thought it was lack of company of his own kind that made him ask this question, but at last, we began to see he was desirous to *avoid* them. When a seaman did stop at the Admiral Benbow (as now and then some did, making for Bristol by the coast road), he would peer into him through the curtained door before entering the parlour; and he was always sure to be as silent as a mouse when any such was present. For me, at least, there was no secret about the matter, for I was, in a way, a sharer in his alarms. He had taken me aside one day and promised me a silver fourpenny on the first of every month if I would only keep my “weather-eye open for a seafaring man with one leg” and let him know the moment he appeared. Often enough when the first of the month arrived and I applied to him for my wages, he would merely blow through his nose at me and stare me down, but before the week was out, he was sure to think better of it, bring me my four-penny piece, and repeat his orders to watch for “the seafaring man with one leg.”

How that personage haunted my dreams, I need scarcely tell you. On stormy nights, when the wind shook the four corners of the house and the surf roared along the cove up the cliffs, I would see him in a thousand forms, and with a thousand diabolical expressions. Now the leg would be cut off at the knee, now at the hip; now he was a monstrous sort of creature who had never possessed but that one leg, and it situated right in the middle of his body. To see him leap and run and pursue me over hedge and ditch was the worst of nightmares. And altogether I paid quite dearly for my monthly fourpenny piece, in the shape of these abominable fancies.

But though I was so terrified by the notion of the seafaring man with one leg, I was far less afraid of the captain himself than any other who knew him. There were nights when he consumed a deal more rum and water than his head could carry; and then he would sometimes sit and sing his wicked, old, wild sea-songs, minding nobody; but at other times he would call for glasses round and force all the trembling company to listen to his tales or bear chorus to his singing. Often I have heard the house shaking with “Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum,” all the neighbours joining in for dear life, with the fear of death upon them, and each singing louder than the other to avoid remark. For in these fits he was the most overriding companion ever known; he would slap his hand on the table for silence all around; he would fly up in a passion of anger at a question, or sometimes because none was posed, and thus judged the company was not following his narrative. Nor would he allow anyone to leave the inn till he had drunk himself sleepy and reeled off to bed.

His stories were what frightened people worst of all. Dreadful tales they were—of hangings, and walking the plank, and storms at sea, and the Dry Tortugas, and wild deeds and places upon the Spanish Main. By his own

reviews.text: Edição excelente traduzida localmente por IA. A leitura flui de forma incrível no e-reader da página!

title: O Livro da Selva
author: Rudyard Kipling
synopsis: Na vibrante selva indiana, onde cada folha sussurra segredos e os animais governam sob leis ancestrais, surge Mowgli, o menino-lobito. Abandonado em meio à mata, ele é acolhido pela alcateia de lobos Bagheera e Baloo, aprendendo as rígidas regras da "Lei da Selva". Mas a vida na floresta é um palco de perigos: o temido tigre Shere Khan ameaça a ordem, e os conflitos entre tribos selvagens são constantes. Mowgli deve navegar por essa tapeçaria selvagem — equilibrando sua natureza humana com seu espírito indomável — para sobreviver, honrar seus laços e garantir que a paz reine sob o olhar vigilante da floresta.
fullText:
"Fora!" sibilou o Pai Lobo. "Saia e caçe com seu mestre. Você já causou danos suficientes por uma noite."

"Vou," disse Tabaqui em voz baixa. "Vocês podem ouvir Shere Khan lá embaixo, nos arbustos. Eu poderia ter poupado a mim mesmo a mensagem."

O Pai Lobo escutou, e abaixo, no vale que descia até um pequeno rio, ele ouviu o lamento seco, irritado, gutural e cantado de um tigre que não capturou nada e não se importa se toda a selva sabe disso.

"Tolo!" exclamou o Pai Lobo. "Começar uma noite de trabalho com esse barulho! Ele pensa que nossos cervos são como seus touros gordos Waingunga?"

"H’sh. Não é nem touro, nem cervo que ele caça esta noite," disse a Mãe Loba. "É *Homem*."

O lamento mudou para uma espécie de ronronado zumbido que parecia vir de todos os quadrantes da bússola. Era o ruído que confunde lenhadores e ciganos dormindo ao relento, e faz com que corram às vezes direto para a boca do tigre.

"Homem!" disse o Pai Lobo, exibindo todos os seus dentes brancos. "Pff! Não há insetos e sapos suficientes nos tanques para ele ter que comer Homem, e também em nossa terra!"

A Lei da Selva, que nunca ordena algo sem um motivo, proíbe toda criatura de comer Homem, exceto quando está matando para mostrar aos seus filhotes *como* matar; e então ele deve caçar fora das áreas de caça de sua alcateia ou tribo. A verdadeira razão para isso é que matar humanos significa, mais cedo ou mais tarde, a chegada de homens brancos em elefantes, com armas, e centenas de homens pardos com gongo, foguetes e tochas.

Então todos na selva sofrem. O motivo pelo qual as feras se dão entre si é que Homem é o ser vivo mais fraco e indefeso de todos, e é anti-esportivo tocá-lo. Eles também dizem — e é verdade — que os comedores de homens ficam malfeitos e perdem os dentes.

O ronronado aumentou, terminando no rugido completo da investida do tigre.

Então veio um uivo — um uivo não tigrístico — vindo de Shere Khan. "Ele errou," disse a Mãe Loba. "O que é?"

O Pai Lobo correu alguns passos e ouviu Shere Khan resmungando e balbuciando selvagemente enquanto tombava na moita.

"O tolo teve mais senso do que pular em uma fogueira de lenhador, e queimou os pés," disse o Pai Lobo com um grunhido. "Tabaqui está com ele."

"Algo vem subindo a colina," disse a Mãe Loba, mexendo uma das orelhas. "Preparem-se."

Os arbustos farfalharam um pouco na moita, e o Pai Lobo caiu com as nádegas para baixo, pronto para saltar. Então, se você estivesse observando, veria a coisa mais maravilhosa do mundo — o lobo travou no meio do salto. Ele deu seu bote antes de ver o que estava atacando, e então tentou parar a si mesmo. O resultado foi que ele disparou reto no ar por quatro ou cinco pés, pousando quase onde havia saído.

"Homem!" ele sibilou. "Um filhote de homem. Olhe!"

Diretamente à sua frente, agarrado a um galho baixo, estava um bebê pardo nu que conseguia andar — tão macio e com pintinhas quanto qualquer pequeno átomo que alguma vez veio a uma caverna de lobo à noite. Ele olhou para o rosto do Pai Lobo e riu.

"É um filhote de homem?" perguntou a Mãe Loba. "Nunca vi um. Traga-o para cá."

Um Lobo acostumado a cuidar dos seus próprios filhotes pode, se necessário, levar à boca um ovo sem quebrá-lo, e embora as mandíbulas do Pai Lobo tenham fechado bem atrás da criança, nenhum dente arranhou a pele ao depositá-la entre os filhotes.

"Que pequeno! Que nu, e... que corajoso!" disse a Mãe Loba suavemente. O bebê estava se forçando entre os filhotes para chegar perto do pelo quente. "Ahai! Ele está pegando sua refeição com os outros. Então este é um filhote de homem. Ora, houve algum lobo que pudesse gabar-se de ter um filhote de homem entre seus filhos?"

"Já ouvi falar disso de tempos em tempos, mas nunca na nossa Alcateia nem no meu tempo," disse o Pai Lobo. "Ele está totalmente sem pelo, e eu poderia matá-lo com um toque do meu pé. Mas veja, ele olha para cima e não tem medo."

A luz da lua foi bloqueada pela boca da caverna, pois a grande cabeça quadrada e os ombros de Shere Khan estavam projetados na entrada. Tabaqui, atrás dele, estava guinchando: "Meu senhor, meu senhor, ele entrou aqui!"

"Shere Khan nos dá grande honra," disse o Pai Lobo, mas seus olhos estavam muito zangados. "O que Shere Khan precisa?"

"Minha presa. Um filhote de homem veio por este lado," disse Shere Khan. "Seus pais fugiram. Dê-mo a mim."

Shere Khan havia saltado em uma fogueira de lenhador, como o Pai Lobo havia dito, e estava furioso com a dor dos pés queimados. Mas o Pai Lobo sabia que a boca da caverna era estreita demais para um tigre entrar por ela. Mesmo onde ele estava, os ombros e patas dianteiras de Shere Khan estavam apertados pela falta de espaço, como os de um homem se tentasse lutar dentro de um barril.

"Os Lobos são um povo livre," disse o Pai Lobo. "Eles recebem ordens do Chefe da Alcateia, e não de qualquer matador de gado listrado. O filhote de homem é nosso — para matar se quisermos."

"Você escolhe e você não escolhe! Que conversa é essa de escolher? Pelo boi que matei, devo ficar farejando na toca do seu cão por meus devidos direitos? Sou eu, Shere Khan, quem fala!"

O rugido do tigre encheu a caverna com trovão. A Mãe Loba sacudiu-se dos filhotes e saltou para frente, seus olhos, como duas luas verdes na es

en.title: The Jungle Book
en.synopsis: From the dense, verdant heart of India’s jungles emerges a tapestry of wild life and moral awakening. Mowgli, a young human boy raised among the wolves, navigates the primal hierarchy of the jungle—a world governed by ancient laws etched in instinct and loyalty. He is caught between his own burgeoning humanity and the fierce embrace of the pack, constantly shadowed by the manipulative cunning of Shere Khan, the fearsome tiger. Through perilous hunts, whispered wisdom from wise old Baloo, and the steadfast devotion of Bagheera the panther, Mowgli learns that survival demands not just strength, but adherence to the Dharma of the jungle. This is a vibrant epic of innocence confronting savagery, where the wild heart teaches us the profound necessity of belonging.
en.fullText:
i.

“Out!” snapped Father Wolf. “Out and hunt with thy master. Thou hast
done harm enough for one night.”

“I go,” said Tabaqui quietly. “Ye can hear Shere Khan below in the
thickets. I might have saved myself the message.”

Father Wolf listened, and below in the valley that ran down to a little
river he heard the dry, angry, snarly, singsong whine of a tiger who has
caught nothing and does not care if all the jungle knows it.

“The fool!” said Father Wolf. “To begin a night’s work with that noise!
Does he think that our buck are like his fat Waingunga bullocks?”

“H’sh. It is neither bullock nor buck he hunts to-night,” said Mother
Wolf. “It is Man.”

The whine had changed to a sort of humming purr that seemed to come
from every quarter of the compass. It was the noise that bewilders
woodcutters and gypsies sleeping in the open, and makes them run
sometimes into the very mouth of the tiger.

“Man!” said Father Wolf, showing all his white teeth. “Faugh! Are there
not enough beetles and frogs in the tanks that he must eat Man, and on
our ground too!”

The Law of the Jungle, which never orders anything without a reason,
forbids every beast to eat Man except when he is killing to show his
children how to kill, and then he must hunt outside the hunting grounds
of his pack or tribe. The real reason for this is that man-killing
means, sooner or later, the arrival of white men on elephants, with
guns, and hundreds of brown men with gongs and rockets and torches.
Then everybody in the jungle suffers. The reason the beasts give among
themselves is that Man is the weakest and most defenseless of all living
things, and it is unsportsmanlike to touch him. They say too--and it is
true--that man-eaters become mangy, and lose their teeth.

The purr grew louder, and ended in the full-throated “Aaarh!” of the
tiger’s charge.

Then there was a howl--an untigerish howl--from Shere Khan. “He has
missed,” said Mother Wolf. “What is it?”

Father Wolf ran out a few paces and heard Shere Khan muttering and
mumbling savagely as he tumbled about in the scrub.

“The fool has had no more sense than to jump at a woodcutter’s campfire,
and has burned his feet,” said Father Wolf with a grunt. “Tabaqui is
with him.”

“Something is coming uphill,” said Mother Wolf, twitching one ear. “Get
ready.”

The bushes rustled a little in the thicket, and Father Wolf dropped
with his haunches under him, ready for his leap. Then, if you had been
watching, you would have seen the most wonderful thing in the world--the
wolf checked in mid-spring. He made his bound before he saw what it was
he was jumping at, and then he tried to stop himself. The result was
that he shot up straight into the air for four or five feet, landing
almost where he left ground.

“Man!” he snapped. “A man’s cub. Look!”

Directly in front of him, holding on by a low branch, stood a naked
brown baby who could just walk--as soft and as dimpled a little atom
as ever came to a wolf’s cave at night. He looked up into Father Wolf’s
face, and laughed.

“Is that a man’s cub?” said Mother Wolf. “I have never seen one. Bring
it here.”

A Wolf accustomed to moving his own cubs can, if necessary, mouth an egg
without breaking it, and though Father Wolf’s jaws closed right on the
child’s back not a tooth even scratched the skin as he laid it down
among the cubs.

“How little! How naked, and--how bold!” said Mother Wolf softly. The
baby was pushing his way between the cubs to get close to the warm hide.
“Ahai! He is taking his meal with the others. And so this is a man’s
cub. Now, was there ever a wolf that could boast of a man’s cub among
her children?”

“I have heard now and again of such a thing, but never in our Pack or in
my time,” said Father Wolf. “He is altogether without hair, and I
could kill him with a touch of my foot. But see, he looks up and is not
afraid.”

The moonlight was blocked out of the mouth of the cave, for Shere Khan’s
great square head and shoulders were thrust into the entrance. Tabaqui,
behind him, was squeaking: “My lord, my lord, it went in here!”

“Shere Khan does us great honor,” said Father Wolf, but his eyes were
very angry. “What does Shere Khan need?”

“My quarry. A man’s cub went this way,” said Shere Khan. “Its parents
have run off. Give it to me.”

Shere Khan had jumped at a woodcutter’s campfire, as Father Wolf had
said, and was furious from the pain of his burned feet. But Father Wolf
knew that the mouth of the cave was too narrow for a tiger to come in
by. Even where he was, Shere Khan’s shoulders and forepaws were cramped
for want of room, as a man’s would be if he tried to fight in a barrel.

“The Wolves are a free people,” said Father Wolf. “They take orders from
the Head of the Pack, and not from any striped cattle-killer. The man’s
cub is ours--to kill if we choose.”

“Ye choose and ye do not choose! What talk is this of choosing? By the
bull that I killed, am I to stand nosing into your dog’s den for my fair
dues? It is I, Shere Khan, who speak!”

The tiger’s roar filled the cave with thunder. Mother Wolf shook herself
clear of the cubs and sprang forward, her eyes, like two green moons in
the darkness, facing the blazing eyes of Shere Khan.

“And it is I, Raksha [The Demon], who answers. The man’s cub is mine,
Lungri--mine to me! He shall not be killed. He shall live to run with
the Pack and to hunt with the Pack; and in the end, look you, hunter of
little naked cubs--frog-eater--fish-killer--he shall hunt thee! Now get
hence, or by the Sambhur that I killed (I eat no starved cattle), back
thou goest to thy mother, burned beast of the jungle, lamer than ever
thou camest into the world! Go!”

Father Wolf looked on amazed. He had almost forgotten the days when he
won Mother Wolf in fair fight from five other wolves, when she ran in
the Pack and was not called The Demon for compliment’s sake. Shere Khan
might have faced Father Wolf, but he could not stand up against Mother
Wolf, for he knew that where he was she had all the advantage of the
ground, and would fight to the death. So he backed out of the cave mouth
growling, and when he was clear he shouted:

“Each dog barks in his own yard! We will see what the Pack will say to
this fostering of man-cubs. The cub is mine, and to my teeth he will
come in the end, O bush-tailed thieves!”

Mother Wolf threw herself down panting among the cubs, and Father Wolf
said to her gravely:

“Shere Khan speaks this much truth. The cub must be shown to the Pack.
Wilt thou still keep him, Mother?”

“Keep him!” she gasped. “He came naked, by night, alone and very hungry;
yet he was not afraid! Look, he has pushed one of my babes to one side
already. And that lame butcher would have killed him and would have run
off to the Waingunga while the villagers here hunted through all our
lairs in revenge! Keep him? Assuredly I will keep him. Lie still, little
frog. O thou Mowgli--for Mowgli the Frog I will call thee--the time will
come when thou wilt hunt Shere Khan as he has hunted thee.”

“But what will our Pack say?” said Father Wolf.

The Law of the Jungle lays down very clearly that any wolf may, when he
marries, withdraw from the Pack he belongs to. But as soon as his cubs
are old enough to stand on their feet he must bring them to the Pack
Council, which is generally held once a month at full moon, in order
that the other wolves may identify them. After that inspection the cubs
are free to run where they please, and until they have killed their
first buck no excuse is accepted if a grown wolf of the Pack kills one
of them. The punishment is death where the murderer can be found; and if
you think for a minute you will see that this must be so.

Father Wolf waited till his cubs could run a little, and then on the
night of the Pack Meeting took them and Mowgli and Mother Wolf to the
Council Rock--a hilltop covered with stones and boulders where a hundred
wolves could hide. Akela, the great gray Lone Wolf, who led all the Pack
by strength and cunning, lay out at full length on his rock, and
below him sat forty or more wolves of every size and color, from
badger-colored veterans who could handle a buck alone to young black
three-year-olds who thought they could. The Lone Wolf had led them for a
year now. He had fallen twice into a wolf trap in his youth, and once he
had been beaten and left for dead; so he knew the manners and customs
of men. There was very little talking at the Rock. The cubs tumbled over
each other in the center of the circle where their mothers and fathers
sat, and now and again a senior wolf would go quietly up to a cub, look
at him carefully, and return to his place on noiseless feet. Sometimes a
mother would push her cub far out into the moonlight to be sure that
he had not been overlooked. Akela from his rock would cry: “Ye know
the Law--ye know the Law. Look well, O Wolves!” And the anxious mothers
would take up the call: “Look--look well, O Wolves!”

At last--and Mother Wolf’s neck bristles lifted as the time came--Father
Wolf pushed “Mowgli the Frog,” as they called him, into the center,
where he sat laughing and playing with some pebbles that glistened in
the moonlight.

Akela never raised his head from his paws, but went on with the
monotonous cry: “Look well!” A muffled roar came up from behind the
rocks--the voice of Shere Khan crying: “The cub is mine. Give him to
me. What have the Free People to do with a man’s cub?” Akela never even
twitched his ears. All he said was: “Look well, O Wolves! What have
the Free People to do with the orders of any save the Free People? Look
well!”

There was a chorus of deep growls, and a young wolf in his fourth year
flung back Shere Khan’s question to Akela: “What have the Free People to
do with a man’s cub?” Now, the Law of the Jungle lays down that if there
is any dispute as to the right of a cub to be accepted by the Pack, he
must be spoken for by at least two members of the Pack who are not his
father and mother.

“Who speaks for this cub?” said Akela. “Among the Free People who
speaks?” There was no answer and Mother Wolf got ready for what she knew
would be her last fight, if things came to fighting.

Then the only other creature who is allowed at the Pack Council--Baloo,
the sleepy brown bear who teaches the wolf cubs the Law of the Jungle:
old Baloo, who can come and go where he pleases because he eats only
nuts and roots and honey--rose upon his hind quarters and grunted.

“The man’s cub--the man’s cub?” he said. “I speak for the man’s cub.
There is no harm in a man’s cub. I have no gift of words, but I speak
the truth. Let him run with the Pack, and be entered with the others. I
myself will teach him.”

“We need yet another,” said Akela. “Baloo has spoken, and he is our
teacher for the young cubs. Who speaks besides Baloo?”

A black shadow dropped down into the circle. It was Bagheera the Black
Panther, inky black all over, but with the panther markings showing
up in certain lights like the pattern of watered silk. Everybody knew
Bagheera, and nobody cared to cross his path; for he was as cunning as
Tabaqui, as bold as the wild buffalo, and as reckless as the wounded
elephant. But he had a voice as soft as wild honey dripping from a tree,
and a skin softer than down.

“O Akela, and ye the Free People,” he purred, “I have no right in your
assembly, but the Law of the Jungle says that if there is a doubt which
is not a killing matter in regard to a new cub, the life of that cub may
be bought at a price. And the Law does not say who may or may not pay
that price. Am I right?”

“Good! Good!” said the young wolves, who are always hungry. “Listen to
Bagheera. The cub can be bought for a price. It is the Law.”

“Knowing that I have no right to speak here, I ask your leave.”

“Speak then

pt-br.title: O Livro da Selva
pt-br.synopsis: Na vibrante selva indiana, onde cada folha sussurra segredos e os animais governam sob leis ancestrais, surge Mowgli, o menino-lobito. Abandonado em meio à mata, ele é acolhido pela alcateia de lobos Bagheera e Baloo, aprendendo as rígidas regras da "Lei da Selva". Mas a vida na floresta é um palco de perigos: o temido tigre Shere Khan ameaça a ordem, e os conflitos entre tribos selvagens são constantes. Mowgli deve navegar por essa tapeçaria selvagem — equilibrando sua natureza humana com seu espírito indomável — para sobreviver, honrar seus laços e garantir que a paz reine sob o olhar vigilante da floresta.
